prior.title: Iowa Department of Transportation Freight Planning and Transportation Systems Overview
prior.summary: The Iowa DOT provides comprehensive freight planning resources including state freight and rail plans, multi-modal transportation coordination, and freight advisory services. The page outlines Iowa's freight transportation system encompassing airports, highways, pipelines, railroads, and waterways as critical to state economic competitiveness and regional/national freight movements.
prior.full_brief:
# Iowa Freight Transportation System — Operations Profile

**Scope:** This brief synthesises the Iowa Department of Transportation's freight planning framework, the 2022 State Freight Plan, the 2026 draft State Freight Plan update, the Iowa Freight Advisory Council structure, the 2025 Iowa State Rail Plan, the Iowa in Motion 2050 State Transportation Plan, and FHWA's national freight plan compliance registry. It is oriented toward workspaces operating freight forwarding, logistics, or supply-chain services across Iowa's multi-modal network — road-primary, with rail and waterway as secondary modes — handling agriculture, energy, industrial, and distribution cargo verticals.

**Convention notes:** FACT claims are cited inline and span-grounded to the verbatim source text provided. ANALYSIS and LEGAL claims are labeled. No cost figures, carrier rates, or fuel prices appear in the source blocks provided; those slots are explicitly gapped rather than fabricated. Contact names and phone numbers appearing in source blocks are Iowa DOT staff; they are not workspace personnel and are referenced only as public agency contacts. Dates are current as of the generation timestamp.

---

# Operational Cost Baseline for the Region

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-28: None of the seven source blocks provided contains sourced industrial electricity rates, diesel prices, drayage rates, port handling fees, or labor cost figures for Iowa. Current rate data is not publicly available from the primary sources in scope. Workspaces should obtain current figures from Iowa-specific tariff filings, U.S. EIA State Energy Profiles (Iowa), ATRI operational cost surveys, and the Iowa DOT Five-Year Highway Program. These are listed under New Sources Identified as research leads.*

*Specific operational cost figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

---

# Feasibility of Specific Operational Choices

## Multi-Modal Access: Highway, Rail, Waterway, Air

Iowa's transportation system is explicitly described as comprising airports, highways, pipelines, railroads, and waterways, all of which "play a major role in supporting the state's economy." *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight

**Road:** Highway is the primary freight mode. The Iowa DOT operates an oversize and overweight permit system, IFTA (International Fuel Tax Agreement) compliance, and IRP (International Registration Plan) registration — all active and accessible to motor carriers. *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight

**Rail:** The 2025 Iowa State Rail Plan is the active governing document. *Analytical inference:* The plan's explicit focus on "promoting access to rail transportation, helping to improve the freight railroad transportation system" signals that rail remains a feasible secondary mode for bulk and industrial cargo, though capacity and interchange details require carrier-level confirmation. *Source: Iowa State Rail Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2025. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/rail-plan

Rail operators with active representation on the Iowa Freight Advisory Council (FAC) include CPKC Railroad (Ryan Nonnemaker, Director of U.S. Government Affairs), Union Pacific Railroad (Kelli O'Brien, Director of Public Affairs for IA, MN, and WI), and Iowa Interstate Railroad (Joe Parsons, Chief Operating Officer). *Source: Freight Advisory Council Member List, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council

**Waterway:** Waterway access is confirmed as part of Iowa's transportation system. The Port of Blencoe is referenced through FAC membership (Frank Huseman, Operations Manager, NEW Cooperative/Port of Blencoe), and the Upper Mississippi River inland waterway was the subject of an alternative financing study in 2019. *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers maintains representation on the FAC through both the Rock Island District (Tom Heinold) and the Omaha District (Lt. Col. Charles McWilliams), covering both the Mississippi and Missouri River corridors. *Source: Freight Advisory Council Member List, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces moving bulk agricultural or energy commodities, barge access via the Upper Mississippi and Missouri River systems represents a cost-competitive alternative to road and rail for high-volume, non-time-sensitive moves. Feasibility is corridor- and commodity-specific; rate availability requires direct carrier inquiry.

**Air:** Aviation is listed as a component of Iowa's transportation system and the Iowa DOT administers an Aviation Plan and airport improvement programs. *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight Specific air cargo capacity figures and airport freight throughput data are not available from the source blocks provided.

*Specific air cargo throughput and capacity figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

## Oversize and Overweight Permitting

The Iowa DOT operates an active oversize and overweight permit system with online application capability. Permit types and requirements are published. *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight *Analytical inference:* For workspaces moving heavy industrial equipment, large-format event infrastructure, or oversized cargo (consistent with live events, film and TV, or high-value automotive verticals), the Iowa permit system is an established pathway — but dimensional limits and corridor restrictions require permit-specific confirmation.

---

# Cost Comparison Against Alternatives

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-28: The source blocks do not contain breakeven analysis, payback calculations, modal cost comparisons, or sourced rate differentials for Iowa. The 2022 State Freight Plan's Chapter 6 (referenced in the source blocks as "Projected costs, revenues by mode, and strategies to address funding shortfalls") may contain relevant figures but the full document text was not provided in the source pool. The FHWA Freight Planning toolkit and the Iowa DOT Five-Year Highway Program are identified as candidate sources; see New Sources Identified.*

*Specific modal cost comparison figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

---

# Cross-Regional Strategic Implications

## Iowa as a Through-State Corridor

A material volume of freight moving through Iowa originates outside the state: "a significant amount of freight originates outside of Iowa and moves through the state to destinations in other states." *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight

*Operational implication:* For workspaces routing east–west or north–south intermodal freight across the Midwest, Iowa's highway and rail network functions as a transit corridor, not only an origin-destination market. This expands the strategic relevance of Iowa freight planning beyond Iowa-domiciled shippers.

## Federal Reauthorization Alignment

Recent Federal Transportation Reauthorization Acts have "placed increasing emphasis on the importance of the efficient movement of freight and encourage States to consider freight movements in their overall planning processes." *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight

*Analytical inference:* Federal freight emphasis increases the probability that Iowa DOT investment priorities — surfaced in the Five-Year Highway Program and State Freight Plan — align with federally designated freight network projects, which in turn affects access to FHWA freight formula funding and the BUILD/INFRA grant programs. Workspaces operating on corridors that intersect federally designated freight routes may benefit from infrastructure investment cycles driven by this alignment.

## Multi-State Rail Connections: CPKC and Union Pacific

CPKC Railroad and Union Pacific Railroad both hold FAC membership. *Source: Freight Advisory Council Member List, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council *Analytical inference:* CPKC's Trans-America corridor and Union Pacific's midwest network mean that Iowa rail connects to Gulf, Pacific, and Canadian border gateways — a cross-regional implication for workspaces sourcing or delivering into Iowa agricultural and industrial supply chains. Specific interchange and intermodal ramp locations are not confirmed from the source blocks.

## Commodity Dependency: Agriculture and Energy

The FAC membership includes representation from Iowa Farm Bureau, Landus Cooperative, Sukup Manufacturing, NEW Cooperative, and Agribusiness Association of Iowa, alongside energy logistics operator Travero (the FAC Chair's employer). *Source: Freight Advisory Council Member List, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council *Analytical inference:* Iowa's freight demand is structurally weighted toward agricultural commodities (grain, fertilizer, equipment) and energy (ethanol, wind energy components). Workspaces servicing distribution, logistics, or industrial supply chains in Iowa will encounter seasonal freight demand peaks driven by harvest cycles and planting seasons, and year-round demand driven by energy infrastructure buildout.

---

# Competitive Positioning in the Region

## Iowa DOT's Planning Framework as a Procurement Signal

The State Freight Plan is "one of a variety of additional plans and tools the department utilizes to ultimately inform the development of the Five-Year Program." *Source: Iowa State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2022. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/freight-plan *Analytical inference:* The Five-Year Highway Program is the capital deployment instrument. Workspaces or their clients whose freight corridors align with Five-Year Program investment priorities gain access to improved infrastructure ahead of corridors that are not prioritized. Monitoring the Five-Year Program update cycle creates an early signal for route-level infrastructure changes.

## FAC as a Stakeholder Access Point

The FAC was "created in 2012 as a forum to assist with understanding the complexities associated with freight movements through education, discussion, and review." Members represent agriculture, energy, distribution, logistics, and multimodal transportation, as well as local and state government agencies, and "meet on a quarterly basis to address critical topics cooperatively identified by the FAC Chair and Iowa DOT staff." *Source: Freight Advisory Council, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council

*Analytical inference:* Participation in or direct engagement with FAC-affiliated operators (Amazon DSM5, Ruan Transportation, Decker Truck Lines, Iowa 80 Group, Perishable Distributors of Iowa) gives workspaces insight into freight mobility priorities before they are formalized in plans and investment programs. The FAC's quarterly meeting cadence produces publicly available meeting materials — a low-cost intelligence source.

## Named Operators Active in Iowa

The FAC membership provides the following named operator-level participants (all citations: *Source: Freight Advisory Council Member List, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight/freight-advisory-council):

- **Ruan Transportation** — Marty Wadle, Chief Commercial Officer, FAC Vice-Chair. Iowa-based transportation and logistics operator.
- **Decker Truck Lines** — Bob Elkins, Chief Operating Officer. Iowa-based truckload carrier.
- **Iowa 80 Group** — Delia Moon-Meier, Senior Vice President. Operator of truck stop and logistics services on I-80.
- **Perishable Distributors of Iowa** — Joe McConnell, Vice President of Transportation. Temperature-controlled distribution operator.
- **Amazon DSM5** — Doug Martin, Assistant General Manager. Amazon distribution center in Des Moines metro.
- **Travero** — Jillian Walsh, Senior Business Development Manager, FAC Chair. Multi-modal logistics operator (formerly Cargill's transportation business).
- **Ten D, Inc. / Companies/Merchants Distribution Service** — Greg Dickinson, President. Distribution services.
- **Des Moines Industrial** — Gabe Claypool, President.
- **John Deere Des Moines Works** — Matt Leyser, Manager of Factory Order Fulfillment. Heavy equipment manufacturing and outbound logistics.

*Analytical inference:* These named operators represent the active freight market in Iowa across trucking, temperature-controlled distribution, heavy equipment, agricultural supply chain, and e-commerce fulfillment. Workspaces positioning for Iowa freight business will compete or partner within this operator set. No sourced data on market share, contract terms, or competitive positioning beyond membership affiliation is available from the source blocks.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

## What the Workspace Can Credibly Say

1. **Iowa's multi-modal system is structurally intact and state-prioritized.** The Iowa DOT operates highway, rail, waterway, and aviation freight planning as an integrated system, guided by a federally approved State Freight Plan and a 30-year state transportation plan (Iowa in Motion 2050). *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight

2. **Federal freight funding alignment is active.** The State Freight Plan has been formally approved by FHWA. *Source: Iowa State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2022. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/freight-plan *Analytical inference:* FHWA approval signals eligibility for freight formula funding allocations — relevant to clients evaluating infrastructure-dependent supply chains in Iowa.

3. **Iowa is a transit corridor, not just a destination.** "A significant amount of freight originates outside of Iowa and moves through the state to destinations in other states." *Source: Iowa DOT Freight Overview, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/freight This is a credible talking point for clients evaluating Iowa as a consolidation or transload location.

4. **The 2026 freight plan update is underway.** The Iowa DOT has published a draft State Freight Plan for public comment. *Source: Iowa DOT Invites Comments on the Draft State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, February 24, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/news/2026-02-24/iowa-dot-invites-comments-draft-state-freight-plan Workspaces can credibly represent awareness of this update cycle to clients as evidence of active market intelligence.

## Claims to Avoid

- Do not claim specific Iowa freight rates, lane costs, or modal cost comparisons without a sourced figure — none are available in the source pool.
- Do not characterize Iowa's infrastructure as "excellent" or "deficient" without reference to a specific plan section; the State Freight Plan's performance reporting (Chapter 2) addresses this but the full text was not provided.
- Do not represent FAC membership as implying an endorsement of any specific operator's service quality. Membership is an advisory role, not a procurement credential.

## Questions to Pose to Iowa-Based Clients

- Which commodity verticals does your Iowa freight move support — agriculture, energy, or industrial? (Modal and seasonal implications differ materially.)
- Are your Iowa volumes origin-destination or through-freight? (Corridor planning implications differ.)
- Does your operation use rail interchange at any Iowa point? (2025 Rail Plan may affect interchange priorities.)
- Are any of your Iowa shipments oversize or overweight? (Permit system is active and online; lead time should be confirmed.)

---

# Pending Changes That Shift the Calculus MONITORING

## 2026 Iowa State Freight Plan Update — Public Comment Phase Active

**Status:** The Iowa DOT published the draft State Freight Plan for public comment on February 24, 2026, with a comment deadline of April 9, 2026. *Source: Iowa DOT Invites Comments on the Draft State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, February 24, 2026. https://iowadot.gov/news/2026-02-24/iowa-dot-invites-comments-draft-state-freight-plan

**Process context:** The 2022 plan "was presented for information to the Iowa Transportation Commission on May 10, 2022 and then forwarded to the Federal Highway Administration for approval. The plan was formally approved on August 18, 2022." *Source: Iowa State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2022. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/freight-plan

*Analytical inference:* Based on the 2022 cycle, the 2026 plan will follow a similar path: Transportation Commission presentation, then FHWA submission, then formal approval. The comment window closed April 9, 2026; the Transportation Commission presentation and FHWA submission are the next expected milestones. No specific dates for those steps are confirmed in the source blocks.

*Analytical inference:* The new plan will update investment priorities, freight performance measures, and strategies. Workspaces whose clients have Iowa corridor exposure should monitor the plan's final version for changes to prioritized freight corridors, commodity focus areas, and capital investment targets.

**Re-check window:** Revisit when the Iowa Transportation Commission presentation date is announced (expected mid-2026 based on the 2022 precedent) and again at FHWA approval.

## 2025 Iowa State Rail Plan — New Plan Published

A new 2025 Iowa State Rail Plan is now the active rail planning document, replacing the 2022 version. It is described as intended "to guide the Iowa Department of Transportation in its activities of promoting access to rail transportation, helping to improve the freight railroad transportation system, expanding passenger rail service, and promoting improved safety both on the rail system and where the rail system interacts with people and other transportation modes." *Source: Iowa State Rail Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2025. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/rail-plan

*Analytical inference:* The 2025 Rail Plan's freight railroad profiles (covering "overview, transloading, intermodal, interchange, commodity, and subdivision information for freight railroads") represent updated intelligence on Iowa rail capacity, interchange points, and commodity flows. Workspaces routing rail freight through Iowa should obtain the full document to identify whether intermodal or transloading investments have shifted since 2022.

**Re-check window:** Obtain and review the 2025 Rail Plan full document. Monitor for any capital program announcements flowing from Chapter 5 (Rail Service and Investment Program).

## Iowa in Motion 2050 — 2024 Administrative Modification

The State Transportation Plan adopted in May 2022 received an administrative modification in 2024 "to incorporate the following three implementation activities that had occurred since plan adoption: the Carbon Reduction Strategy, Resilience Improvement Plan, and Transportation 4.0: Innovative strategies for the transportation revolution." *Source: State Transportation Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2024. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/state-transportation-plan

*Analytical inference:* The incorporation of a Carbon Reduction Strategy into Iowa's state transportation plan is a forward signal for workspaces with clients monitoring Scope 3 emissions or seeking low-carbon freight routing. The strategy's specific targets and modal implications are not detailed in the source blocks; the full document should be reviewed. The "Transportation 4.0" addition signals that autonomous and connected vehicle technology is now part of Iowa's long-range planning framework — relevant for workspaces evaluating drayage and last-mile technology on Iowa corridors.

**Re-check window:** Review the Carbon Reduction Strategy and Transportation 4.0 documents (both listed as related links on the State Transportation Plan page) for operational specifics.

## Federal Freight Plan Compliance — Quadrennial Update Cycle

The Iowa State Freight Plan "is the second in the current series of freight plans that are now federally required to be updated every four years." *Source: Iowa State Freight Plan, Iowa Department of Transportation, 2022. https://iowadot.gov/transportation-development/systems-planning/areas-planning/transportation-plans/freight-plan FHWA "monitors and approves all State Freight Plan updates to ensure compliance with current federal surface transportation laws." *Source: FHWA Freight Professional Capacity Building Program, Federal Highway Administration, 2026. https://ops.fhwa.dot.gov/freight/fpcb/toolkit/allplans.aspx

*Operational implication:* The 2026 plan update is the third cycle. FHWA approval gates access to freight formula funding. Workspaces whose clients depend on federally funded Iowa freight infrastructure should monitor the FHWA approval timeline as a signal for when new investment priorities become binding on the Five-Year Program.

---

| FHWA Freight Formula Program (NHFP) | https://www.fhwa.dot.gov/fastact/factsheets/nhfpfs.cfm | 1 | Federal source for National Highway Freight Program formula funding allocations by state; links Iowa DOT plan approval to actual funding levels available for corridor investment. |

---
prior.what_it_changes: Iowa's 2026 freight plan update cycle and 2025 Rail Plan set new investment priorities — monitor for corridor-level infrastructure shifts affecting routing decisions.
